title: AI, Grayson Perry, and the Future of Work
content:
In many households, intense debates about artificial intelligence perfectly capture the wide generational divide regarding new technology. Older generations often believe AI is inherently dangerous and that society would be better off relying on traditional, analog methods. Meanwhile, technologically literate youth consistently insist that AI is a mind-blowing tool that will positively revolutionize everything. Despite this widespread skepticism, some professionals do not feel as personally threatened by AI automation as perhaps they should. Fortunately, a few highly creative individuals have ended up in jobs that artificial intelligence is incredibly unlikely to easily steal.

Many writers have seen AI attempt automated parodies of their work, and the results are consistently hopeless and artificial. Human unpredictability and disorganized thought processes, once considered weaknesses, are now seen as superpowers that future-proof creative careers. Similarly, it is hard to imagine AI having much luck replacing unique cultural figures and highly eccentric artists. Their complex, nuanced personas and authentic personal backgrounds provide a fresh perspective that machines simply cannot replicate. Ultimately, the very imperfections and emotional depth of human nature serve as a protective barrier against total artistic automation.

However, recent documentary explorations into the expanding world of AI have yielded somewhat mixed and confusing results. These programs often feature interviews with various figures, from wealthy tech executives to off-grid preppers building remote survival complexes. Skilled interviewers generally excel at asking the cheeky, uncomfortable questions that ordinary viewers are secretly thinking. When interviewing people in romantic relationships with AI avatars, they insightfully question the deeply artificial nature of these customized companions. Unfortunately, the eccentric personalities of the television hosts sometimes become an unhelpful distraction from the serious technological subject at hand.
